data.title: DoorDash Stock Gives Q4 Whiplash, Analysts Defend Bull Case
data.description:
DoorDash, Inc. (NASDAQ:DASH) shares were volatile Thursday as investors weighed top and bottom line Q4 misses against robust growth in Marketplace Gross Order Volume (GOV). 

  • DASH stock is moving. See the chart and price action here. 

The delivery giant reported quarterly earnings of 48 cents per share, missing the analyst consensus of 60 cents. Revenue reached $3.96 billion, marking a 38% year-over-year increase, yet fell short of the $3.99 billion anticipated by Wall Street.

DoorDash stock fell immediately following the report on Wednesday, recovered by double-digits Thursday morning and then slid again throughout the trading session.

Expert Ideas

Both Guggenheim and BofA Securities maintained a Buy rating on DoorDash, but the firms offered differing perspectives on the stock’s valuation and focus areas. 

Despite the headline misses, analyst reports from Guggenheim and BofA Securities highlighted underlying strength in DoorDash’s core operations, and the firms remain largely bullish on the company’s long-term trajectory. 

Guggenheim analyst Michael Morris highlighted DoorDash's steady progress on its 2026 investment roadmap, despite lowering its price target due to broader market conditions.

BofA analyst Justin Post viewed DoorDash's Q4 report as a “clearing event” for margin fears, raising its price objective based on improving efficiency in new business lines.

Photo: Diego Thomazini via Shutterstock

data.title: Here's How Much You Would Have Made Owning KLA Stock In The Last 15 Years
data.description:
KLA (NASDAQ:KLAC) has outperformed the market over the past 15 years by 13.88% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 25.46%. Currently, KLA has a market capitalization of $192.67 billion.

Buying $1000 In KLAC: If an investor had bought $1000 of KLAC stock 15 years ago, it would be worth $30,763.92 today based on a price of $1469.90 for KLAC at the time of writing.

KLA's Performance Over Last 15 Years

Finally -- what's the point of all this? The key insight to take from this article is to note how much of a difference compounded returns can make in your cash growth over a period of time.
